stories.headline: Avalanche reacquire Stanley Cup champion Nazem Kadri from Calgary Flames ahead of trade deadline
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche finalized a deal to bring back center Nazem Kadri from the Calgary Flames just before Friday's NHL trade deadline, reuniting the 35-year-old with the team he helped win the Stanley Cup in 2022. Calgary will retain 20% of Kadri's contract, reducing his cap hit to $5.6 million for the Avalanche. Colorado also receives a 2027 fourth-round pick in the deal.

In return, the Avalanche sent forward Victor Olofsson, prospect Max Curran, a conditional 2028 first-round pick (reported to be top-10 protected) and a conditional 2027 second-round pick to Calgary. Kadri posted 12 goals and 41 points in 61 games this season with the Flames.

The trade gives Colorado extraordinary depth down the middle alongside Nathan MacKinnon, Brock Nelson and recently acquired Nicolas Roy. The Avalanche also added defensemen Nick Blankenburg and Brett Kulak earlier in the week, signaling an aggressive push toward a second Stanley Cup title.

stories.headline: Friday snowstorm dumps 7.5 inches at DIA, snarls metro commute and cancels dozens of flights
stories.summary:
A winter storm rolled through the Denver metro area Friday, dropping 7.5 inches of snow at Denver International Airport by early evening and triggering a Winter Weather Advisory through 8 p.m. for much of the Front Range. The National Weather Service forecast 3 to 5 inches for the city of Denver, with western and southern suburbs potentially seeing 4 to 6 inches.

DIA reported more than 1,000 flight delays and 41 cancellations by mid-afternoon, and a United Airlines flight was temporarily grounded after striking a deicing truck on the tarmac. Numerous schools and government offices closed for the day, and Colorado State Patrol closed northbound I-25 at the Wyoming border during the morning commute due to blowing snow, though it reopened by early afternoon.

Highway 285 between Fairplay and Grant was shut down due to multiple crashes and stuck semi-trucks. South Metro Fire Rescue urged drivers to reduce speeds after a multi-lane crash closed portions of southbound I-25. Temperatures in Denver hovered near freezing throughout the day, roughly 19 degrees below the historical average high for March 6.

stories.headline: Investigators: Open valve at Henderson terminal caused contamination of 1.6 million gallons of Denver-area gasoline in January
stories.summary:
State investigators determined Friday that a January fuel contamination event affecting dozens of Denver-area gas stations was caused by workers at HF Sinclair's Henderson terminal who failed to close a valve, allowing diesel fuel to mix with regular unleaded gasoline. More than 1.6 million gallons of gasoline were contaminated, of which 365,694 gallons were sold to 49 Front Range stations before the problem was discovered.

More than 1,000 consumers reported vehicle problems — including stalling, sputtering and illuminated check engine lights — after refueling at affected stations, which included locations operated by King Soopers, Costco and Murphy Express. The Colorado Division of Oil and Public Safety fined Sinclair $365,694, or $1 per gallon of contaminated gasoline sold, after working with the state Attorney General's office to craft a penalty beyond the standard $500-per-violation statutory limit.

The improper mixing began around 2 p.m. January 7 but was not discovered until early January 8, after gas station operators alerted the company to irregularities. Sinclair did not notify state regulators of the incident on its own and did not provide a list of affected stations until January 13. As a corrective measure, the company implemented a two-person verification system and checklist for valve operations.

stories.headline: Cherry Hills Village officer shoots car theft suspect during hospital medical exam
stories.summary:
A Cherry Hills Village police officer shot and wounded a car theft suspect Thursday evening after the arrestee "violently attacked" the officer during a medical examination at HCA HealthONE Swedish hospital in Englewood. The suspect had been taken to the facility for medical evaluation after being arrested earlier in the day on suspicion of vehicle theft.

Police Chief Jason Lyons said the attack occurred around 5:30 p.m. and no other patients, visitors or staff were injured. The suspect remains hospitalized and has not been publicly identified. The officer was placed on paid administrative leave per departmental policy.

The 18th Judicial District will conduct an independent investigation of the shooting. Lyons said the incident underscores why officers remain with suspects throughout any medical treatment. "Violence in a health care setting is unacceptable," he said in a statement.

stories.headline: U.S. House committee probes fraud and waste allegations in Colorado's Medicaid program
stories.summary:
The U.S. House Committee on Energy and Commerce has sent a formal letter to Gov. Jared Polis and the Colorado Department of Health Care Policy and Finance demanding information about fraud, waste and abuse in the state's Medicaid program. The March 3 letter, signed by committee chair Rep. Brett Guthrie (R-KY) and two subcommittee chairs, cited concerns that Colorado may be vulnerable to the same kinds of fraudulent billing — including falsified records, identity theft and phantom claims — that have been documented in Minnesota's Medicaid program.

The committee specifically highlighted non-emergency medical transportation spending, which surged 436% between 2019 and 2025 after state officials provided incorrect billing guidance to providers. Investigators also flagged applied behavioral analysis services for children with autism and genetic laboratory services as high-risk areas. State officials have since corrected the transportation guidance, a fix projected to save $60.5 million in fiscal year 2026-27.

Colorado's Medicaid spending has surpassed $14.6 billion — double the amount from a decade ago despite only 7% enrollment growth — with $8.6 billion coming from the federal government. The committee set a March 17 deadline for Colorado's response. Gov. Polis, through a spokesperson, said the state takes Medicaid oversight seriously and is reviewing the letter.

stories.headline: Denver Public Schools community weighs in on proposed policy to restrict ICE cooperation
stories.summary:
Denver Public Schools held a public comment session Thursday on a proposed policy that would designate all district-managed and charter school properties as safe zones, prohibiting employees from cooperating with or assisting U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement. Under the draft policy, school resource officers would be barred from sharing student or family information with federal immigration enforcement agents, and staff could not comply with ICE detainer requests.

Advocates and students testified that fear of immigration enforcement is causing significant mental health strain and keeping some students away from school. Youth Program Manager Nayeli Banuelos of Movimiento Poder said the policy would allow schools to function as spaces of learning rather than fear. Several DPS students described seeing individuals they believed to be ICE agents monitoring school entrances.

Opponents at the meeting argued the district would be creating a harmful environment by shielding people who have violated federal law, and urged DPS to encourage cooperation with law enforcement instead. The proposed policy comes days after the Denver City Council unanimously banned mask-wearing by ICE agents and other law enforcement operating within city limits. No vote date on the DPS policy has been announced.

stories.headline: Colorado measles outbreak grows to 8 cases; exposure sites span DIA, schools, grocery stores and bowling alleys
stories.summary:
Colorado's measles outbreak has grown to eight confirmed cases in 2026, with seven linked to connections at two Broomfield schools — Broomfield High School and Broomfield Heights Middle School. The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment has identified 20 possible exposure sites across Broomfield, Denver, Frederick, Lafayette, Louisville and Westminster, including Denver International Airport, multiple restaurants, health care facilities, a grocery store, a church and a bowling alley.

Measles symptoms can take up to three weeks to appear and include fever, cough, runny nose and red eyes before a characteristic rash develops. Because the rash appears roughly four days after a person becomes contagious, individuals can spread the virus unknowingly. Two doses of the MMR vaccine reduce infection risk by about 97%, and partial protection is possible if a shot is administered within three days of exposure.

Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, a dramatic increase over the prior decade in which annual totals ranged from zero to two. State health officials are urging anyone who may have visited the identified exposure locations to monitor for symptoms and call ahead before seeking medical care to avoid further spread.

stories.headline: Nuggets beat Lakers 120-113 behind Jokic triple-double, despite four key players sidelined
stories.summary:
Nikola Jokic recorded 28 points, 12 rebounds and 13 assists — including a decisive 8-footer with 22.3 seconds remaining — to lead the Denver Nuggets past the Los Angeles Lakers 120-113 on Thursday at Ball Arena. Jamal Murray added 28 points and Julian Strawther contributed 18 in his second consecutive start while filling in for injured teammates.

The Nuggets played without Aaron Gordon, Peyton Watson, Spencer Jones and Cam Johnson but still held off a late Lakers rally fueled by Luka Doncic, who needed 24 shots to score 27 points. Christian Braun played a team-high 40 minutes and effectively contained Doncic during critical late possessions, while new roster addition Tyus Jones made his Denver debut, playing six minutes.

The win moved Denver to 39-24 and kept the Nuggets 1.5 games ahead of the Lakers for fifth place in the Western Conference. The victory was Denver's second straight and came against the NBA's most clutch-time-efficient team, a category in which the Nuggets have historically struggled since Jokic's return from a knee injury.

stories.headline: Federal judge dismisses constitutional challenge to Denver's affordable housing fee ordinances
stories.summary:
A federal judge dismissed a lawsuit Tuesday brought by homebuilder redT Homes and redT Capital Partners challenging the constitutionality of two Denver ordinances requiring developers to contribute to affordable housing. The plaintiffs argued that Denver's linkage fee — charged per square foot on new housing developments with fewer than 10 units — and the mandatory affordable housing ordinance violated the U.S. Constitution's takings clause by imposing fees not proportional to the development's actual impact on housing demand.

U.S. District Court Judge Philip A. Brimmer granted Denver's motion to dismiss in a March 3 order, finding that the mandatory affordable housing ordinance provided developers a constitutional alternative path to compliance by designating a portion of units as affordable housing. The two redT developments at issue faced linkage fees of approximately $25,000 and $45,000 respectively.

Denver's city council enacted the ordinances citing rapid population and job growth that has driven housing demand well beyond supply, particularly for low- and moderate-income workers. The ruling is a significant win for the city's affordable housing strategy, though the plaintiffs could appeal the decision.

stories.headline: Denver police chase stolen vehicle onto I-70 in Aurora, officer injured
stories.summary:
Denver police pursued a stolen vehicle onto eastbound Interstate 70 in Aurora on Thursday after the driver ran over an officer's foot during a traffic stop near 45th Avenue and Zuni Street and fled the scene. Officers forced the vehicle to stop west of Chambers Road, and the driver was arrested and taken to a hospital for evaluation. The officer whose foot was struck was also transported to a hospital.

The chase forced the closure of eastbound I-70 at Interstate 225 beginning around 4:51 p.m., backing up traffic significantly during the evening commute. One lane reopened by 6:15 p.m. and the highway was fully cleared by 7:30 p.m., according to the Colorado Department of Transportation.

The identities of the suspect and the injured officer were not released. No additional details on charges or the extent of the officer's injuries were immediately available.

stories.headline: State patrol releases update on fatal February dust storm pileup involving 30-plus vehicles south of Pueblo
stories.summary:
The Colorado State Patrol on Friday released a preliminary reconstruction of the February 17 dust storm crash on Interstate 25 south of Pueblo that involved more than 30 vehicles. Investigators believe the chain-reaction collision began when a 2016 Ford Escape rear-ended a GMC Sierra traveling 15 to 20 mph in a 75 mph zone under "brown-out" conditions, rotating the Escape into the left lane and setting off a cascading series of crashes.

Subsequent collisions involved a Kenworth semi-truck and a Ram 3500 hauling a stock trailer, whose trailer detached and struck other vehicles. The blockage created by the initial crash triggered additional multi-vehicle impacts, and CSP said the timeline for several involved vehicles remains under investigation.

The crash resulted in multiple fatalities and injuries, the full details of which were included in the patrol's release. Investigators said the crash remains open and further updates will be issued as the investigation continues.

stories.headline: Denver metro home sales surge in February as mortgage rates fall below 6% and inventory rises
stories.summary:
The Denver metro real estate market accelerated sharply in February as mortgage rates dropped below 6% for the first time in months, drawing buyers back into a market that had been sluggish through much of 2025. Pending sales jumped more than 29% from January and were up over 15% year-over-year, according to the Denver Metro Association of Realtors' monthly report.

Median sale prices declined modestly — down 5% for attached homes and 2% for detached homes compared to a year ago — while new listings rose 12% from January, expanding buyer choices. Days on market fell sharply, dropping 30% for attached homes and 40% for detached homes, and the close-price-to-list-price ratio climbed to 98.7%, signaling rising demand. Closed sales were up nearly 30%.

In the luxury segment, closings for homes priced between $1 million and $1.49 million rose 35%, and sales in the $1.5 million to $1.9 million range surged nearly 66%. Despite February's gains, year-to-date 2026 sales volume remains roughly 10% below the same period in 2025, and the median close price is down about 3% year-over-year.

weather.notable: About 12 degrees below the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: The only marathon I'll ever run is a Netflix marathon. And even then, I'll need a break halfway through.

weekendEvents.title: Denver Museum of Nature & Science Free Evening
weekendEvents.description: The Denver Museum of Nature & Science offers free admission as part of an SCFD Free Day. No ticket purchase required during the free admission window.
weekendEvents.location: Denver Museum of Nature & Science, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Denver Restaurant Week
weekendEvents.description: The 22nd annual Denver Restaurant Week features more than 300 participating restaurants offering special multi-course menus at fixed prices ranging from $25 to $55. Includes Michelin-recognized restaurants and eateries from James Beard Award-winning chefs.

weekendEvents.title: Colorado Golf Expo
weekendEvents.description: An expo for golfers of all levels featuring pro demos, new gear, mini golf, and interactive hands-on activities. Free parking included with admission; kids get in free.

weekendEvents.title: Mile High Beer Fest
weekendEvents.description: More than 40 local and national breweries, including Left Hand Brewing Co., Renegade Brewing, and Eddyline Brewery, gather at Denargo Market for unlimited beer samples during general admission hours.

weekendEvents.title: Blarney on Belmar
weekendEvents.description: A free, family-friendly St. Patrick's Day festival featuring live music from The Elders and Tadhg & Friends, Irish step dancers, a Lucky Clover Market, and festive food and drink from local breweries.
weekendEvents.location: Belmar, 439 S. Upham St. (at Alameda Ave. and Wadsworth Blvd.), Lakewood

weekendEvents.title: SeriesFest Fashion in Focus: Molly Rogers
weekendEvents.description: SeriesFest presents Emmy-winning costume designer Molly Rogers, known for HBO's 'Sex and the City,' 'And Just Like That…,' and 'The Devil Wears Prada,' in conversation alongside a runway show featuring more than 40 models wearing iconic TV and film costumes. Rogers will receive SeriesFest's Excellence in Costume Design Award.
weekendEvents.location: The Cable Center, University of Denver, 2000 Buchtel Blvd., Denver

weekendEvents.title: Denver Brass: Celtic Mania
weekendEvents.description: Denver Brass presents its annual Celtic-themed program featuring thunderous brass, bagpipes, and performances from Celtic Colorado Pipes & Drums, Rocky Mountain Highland Dancers, and Celtic Steps Irish Dance School. Three performances across two days.
weekendEvents.location: Newman Center for the Performing Arts, Denver

weekendEvents.title: Leadville Ski Joring
weekendEvents.description: Skiers are pulled by horses and riders through a course of jumps, gates, and ring-collection challenges on Leadville's Main Street. A free spectator event open to all ages; no dogs allowed.

weekendEvents.title: No Man's Land Film Festival
weekendEvents.description: The premier all-woman and gender-nonconforming adventure film festival celebrates athletes and adventurers across adventure, sport, and conservation.
weekendEvents.location: Denver (see event website for venue details)

weekendEvents.title: Molly Brown House Museum Free Day
weekendEvents.description: The historic home of Titanic survivor Margaret 'Molly' Brown offers free admission as part of an SCFD Free Day on International Women's Day.

weekendEvents.title: Demetri Martin: Quick Draw Tour
weekendEvents.description: Stand-up comedian and writer-actor Demetri Martin headlines the Paramount Theatre as part of his Quick Draw Tour, featuring his signature smart one-liners and on-stage drawings.

weekendEvents.title: Los Angeles Lakers at Denver Nuggets
weekendEvents.description: The Denver Nuggets host the Los Angeles Lakers in an NBA regular season game at Ball Arena.

topStory.headline: Trump Demands Iran's 'Unconditional Surrender' as U.S.-Israeli Strikes Enter Second Week
topStory.summary:
President Donald Trump declared Friday that the United States would accept nothing less than Iran's "unconditional surrender," ruling out any negotiated settlement to the ongoing military campaign against Tehran. Writing on his Truth Social platform, Trump said that following surrender and the selection of what he called "acceptable" new leadership, the U.S. and its allies would work to make Iran "economically bigger, better, and stronger than ever before." White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters the administration expects military operations to last four to six weeks.

The conflict, now close to entering its second week, began following the killing of Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. U.S. Central Command reported that in the first seven days of Operation Epic Fury, American forces have struck more than 3,000 targets and damaged or destroyed 43 Iranian ships. On Friday, Israeli forces launched what the Israeli military described as a "broad-scale new wave of strikes" on Mehrabad Airport, Tehran's main domestic hub, with eyewitness footage showing burning aircraft and large plumes of smoke — a significantly heavier bombardment than an earlier Israeli strike on the facility March 4.

A separate and serious development emerged Friday when U.S. investigators said they believe the United States may have been responsible for a deadly airstrike on a girls' primary school in Iran on Feb. 28, the first day of the war. Iranian state media reported the strike killed dozens of people, including schoolgirls ages 7 to 12. Investigators reached that preliminary assessment because the U.S. was operating in the area while Israel was not, though the White House said no final conclusions have been reached and called speculation otherwise "irresponsible and false."

The war has drawn in additional geopolitical complications, with multiple sources reporting that Russia has been providing intelligence to Iran on U.S. military movements in the region. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said in an interview set to air Sunday on "60 Minutes" that the U.S. is "tracking everything" and incorporating the intelligence-sharing into battle plans. A dozen Senate Democrats, including Minority Leader Chuck Schumer, separately criticized Trump for waiving sanctions to allow India to more easily purchase Russian oil, arguing the move enriches Moscow at a time when U.S. strikes have already driven a spike in global energy prices.

Trump has declined to use the term "regime change" in describing U.S. objectives, though he has indicated he intends to play a role in selecting Iran's next leader. He told Axios earlier this week that installing Khamenei's son Mojtaba — seen as close to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps — would be "unacceptable." The White House likened the situation to the U.S. operation in Venezuela earlier this year, in which American forces removed President Nicolás Maduro and the administration backed a chosen successor, a comparison that underscores the administration's willingness to pursue direct intervention in foreign leadership transitions.
